sanskrit_devanagari:
यत्साङ्ख्यैः प्राप्यते स्थानं तद्योगैरपि गम्यते ।
एकं साङ्ख्यं च योगं च यः पश्यति स पश्यति ॥ ५ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
yat sāṅkhyaiḥ prāpyate sthānaṁ tad yogair api gamyate |
ekaṁ sāṅkhyaṁ ca yogaṁ ca yaḥ paśyati sa paśyati || 5 ||

translations.verbatim_quote: One who knows that the position reached by means of analytical study can also be attained by devotional service, and who therefore sees analytical study and devotional service to be on the same level, sees things as they are.

One who knows that the position reached by means of analytical study can also be attained by devotional service, and who therefore sees analytical study and devotional service to be on the same level, sees things as they are.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 1026 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The real purpose of philosophical research is to find the ultimate goal of life. Since the ultimate goal of life is self-realization, there is no difference between the conclusions reached by the two processes.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 5.5 Sthanam, the State called Liberation; yat prapyate, that is reached; sankhyaih, by the Sankhyas, by the monks steadfast in Knowledge; tat prapyate, that is reached; yogaih, by the yogis; api, as well.
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-5-5-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara: the place (sthāna, i.e., liberation) reached by the sāṅkhyas is the same place reached by the yogis. The one who sees sāṅkhya and yoga as one (in their fruit) sees rightly. Shankara's reading preserves the convergence at the destination while distinguishing the practices.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 5.5 The fruit in the form of the vision of the self which is attained by the Sankhyans (i.e.) Jnana Yogins, the same is attained alone by those who are Karma Yogins. He alone is wise who sees that Sankhya and the Yoga are one and the same because of their having the same result.
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-5-5-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja: the state attained by the followers of jñāna-yoga is also reached by the followers of karma-yoga. The one who sees both paths as one sees correctly. For Ramanuja the unity is in the soteriological function — both are means to the self's release.

disagreements_among_translators.word: ekaṁ sāṅkhyaṁ ca yogaṁ ca yaḥ paśyati

positions.rendering: sees analytical study and devotional service to be on the same level
sees the two paths as converging in their fruit (oneness of destination)
sees the two paths as one in soteriological function

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All converge on 'oneness'. Shankara confines the oneness to fruit; Ramanuja extends it to function; Prabhupada renders idiomatically as 'same level'. None of the three flatten the two paths into mere identity at the level of practice — the practices remain distinct.

literal_meaning: That state which is reached by the sāṅkhyas is reached also by the yogis. The one who sees sāṅkhya and yoga as one — he sees.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna repeats and strengthens 5.4. The sthāna (state, place) reached by jñāna-yogis is the same sthāna reached by karma-yogis. To see them as one is to see correctly. The verse closes the equivalence argument that began at 5.2.
